_meta._file_role: Phase 2 entry #1. The Freed-Hopkins cobordism classification of invertible topological phases. Adds one formal-classification node, bears-on and uses-classification edges, and one cross-classification edge to the ten-fold way (specializes subtype, first use).
_meta._notes: Tests the `group` axis kind (untested in Phase 1) and the `specializes` cross-classification subtype (untested in v15.1 retrofit). Anticipated to surface §10.3 (multi-classification cells) via the SM × anomaly-cobordism relation, which is treated in the findings rather than as a schema feature in this entry.

nodes.label: Freed-Hopkins cobordism
nodes.full_name: Freed-Hopkins cobordism classification of invertible topological field theories and symmetry-protected phases
nodes.description: The classification of invertible topological field theories (and the gapped symmetry-protected topological phases they describe) by cobordism. Freed and Hopkins (2016, 2019) proposed that invertible TQFTs in d spacetime dimensions with tangential structure G (specifying orientation, spin, pin, or twisted variants thereof) are classified by [MTG, Σ^{d+1} IZ] — homotopy classes of maps from the Thom spectrum of G to a shift of the Anderson dual of the sphere spectrum. For most cases of physical interest the classification reduces to the torsion subgroup of the (d+1)-dimensional G-bordism group, Ω^G_{d+1}, often combined with its free part. The classification subsumes earlier classifications as special cases: free fermion topological phases (Kitaev's ten-fold way) are recovered for the appropriate tangential structures (Spin, Pin+, Pin-, etc.); bosonic SPT phases match the group-cohomology classification (Chen-Gu-Wen 2011, 2013) in low dimensions; and several Fidkowski-Kitaev-type interaction-induced collapses are computed structurally rather than discovered case-by-case.
nodes.domain_of_applicability: Invertible ("deformation classes of") topological field theories and the SPT phases they describe, for matter with internal symmetries acting via a tangential-structure group G on spacetime. Covers fermionic and bosonic SPTs with discrete internal symmetries and U(1)-valued symmetries; covers crystalline-symmetry-protected phases via twisted tangential structures (a technical refinement). Excludes: non-invertible topological phases (true topological order with anyonic excitations — classified separately by modular tensor categories); gapless topological phases; phases with anomalous boundary states that are not fully captured by the cobordism invariant. Also excludes the cosmological-scale generalization to quantum gravity (anomaly cobordism for gravitational-with-internal-symmetry combinations is studied but the full classification is incomplete).
nodes.internal_structure: Each tangential structure G is a specific Lie group or topological group describing the geometric structure on spacetime: SO is orientation; Spin is the double cover of SO (required for fermions on orientable manifolds); Pin± are unoriented analogs of Spin (relevant when time-reversal symmetry is included); Spin^c is Spin × U(1) / Z_2 (relevant when charge conservation is included). The (d+1)-dimensional G-bordism group Ω^G_{d+1} contains the classification information for invertible phases in d spatial dimensions (or d+1 spacetime dimensions, depending on convention). Famous identities: Ω^{Pin-}_2 = Z_8 (Fidkowski-Kitaev's 1d BDI collapse); Ω^{Pin+}_4 = Z_16 (3+1d time-reversal-invariant fermionic SPT, refining the ten-fold way's class AII Z_2 entry to a Z_16 interacting classification). The classification's structure-group axis is canonically the `group` axis kind in the v14 schema (first use in Phase 2 entries).

classification_axes.name: tangential-structure
classification_axes.description: The five most-studied tangential structures in the Freed-Hopkins classification. SO: orientation (bosonic, oriented). Spin: orientation + spin structure (fermionic, no further symmetry). Pin+ / Pin-: unoriented spin structures (fermionic with time-reversal symmetry; the two signs distinguish T² = -1 from T² = +1). Spin^c: Spin structure twisted by U(1) (fermionic with charge conservation). Additional tangential structures exist (Spin × Z_2^F for fermion-parity-twisted theories; Spin × G for various internal symmetry groups G); these are not enumerated here. Notation follows Freed-Hopkins 2016 §1 and the standard references.
classification_axes.citation: Freed, Hopkins 2016, arXiv:1604.06527 (published 2019, Comm. Math. Phys. 374)

classification_axes.name: spacetime-dimension
classification_axes.description: Spacetime dimension d+1 in which the invertible field theory lives (equivalently: dimension d+1 of the closed manifold whose G-bordism class is the relevant classifying invariant). Conventions in the literature vary on whether the indexed dimension refers to the SPT's spatial dimension d or the field theory's spacetime dimension d+1; this entry uses spacetime dimension d, with cell content giving Ω^G_d for clarity. Cells populated for d ∈ {0, 1, 2, 3, 4}; higher dimensions extend the classification but are less physically relevant and are omitted for compactness.

cells.description: Oriented points with sign; the integer counts oriented zero-manifolds.
No invertible bosonic SPT phases in 1d.
No invertible bosonic SPT phases in 2d (within the strict-bosonic non-anomalous regime).
No invertible bosonic SPT phases in 3d (without further symmetry refinements).
Classified by the signature of the oriented 4-manifold. The corresponding invertible TQFT is the gravitational theta-term (signature action).
The mod-2 invariant; corresponds to the choice of NS vs R sector in 1d fermionic systems.
Classified by the Arf invariant; the famous 2d Arf-Brown invariant of a quadratic refinement of the intersection form.
Classified by the signature (more precisely, by σ/16 — the Â-genus, an integer for Spin manifolds by Rokhlin's theorem).

The famous Z_16 classification of 3+1d topological insulators with time-reversal symmetry T² = -1. Refines the ten-fold-way class AII entry (Z_2 at the free-fermion level) to Z_16 in the interacting case. The Z_16 captures multi-particle interaction effects that the free-fermion classification cannot.
realized_examples: The general 3+1d topological insulator with T² = -1, when interactions are included. The free-fermion case is one of 16 distinct interacting phases.

predictions.prediction: Time-reversal-invariant 3+1d fermionic SPT phases form a Z_16 classification when interactions are included, refining the Z_2 free-fermion classification of the ten-fold way's class AII.
predictions.prediction_citation: Wang, Senthil 2014, Phys. Rev. B 89, 195124; Freed, Hopkins 2016
predictions.confirmation_citation: Theoretical confirmation via multiple independent calculations; experimental confirmation is partial (the higher-Z_16 phases require specific interaction-induced phases not yet definitively isolated experimentally).

cells.description: The Fidkowski-Kitaev classification: the Z classification of free fermion class BDI in 1d (ten-fold-way's BDI-d1 cell content) collapses to Z_8 when interactions are included. This is the canonical example of the free-to-interacting reach termination that motivated the topological-phases-classification frontier (added in v14.1).
realized_examples: 1d class BDI topological superconductors with strong interactions — the 8 distinct phases collapse from the free-fermion Z classification.

predictions.prediction: The free-fermion Z classification of 1d class BDI topological superconductors collapses to Z_8 under interactions; equivalently, eight free-fermion BDI phases that are distinct without interactions become continuously deformable into one trivial phase when interactions are turned on.
predictions.prediction_citation: Fidkowski, Kitaev 2010, Phys. Rev. B 81, 134509 (first explicit demonstration); Kitaev 2011 talk on classifications; Freed-Hopkins 2016 (cobordism interpretation)
predictions.confirmation_citation: Theoretical confirmation via multiple independent calculations (Turner-Pollmann-Berg 2011; etc.).

cells.description: Classified by the first Chern number; corresponds to the integer quantum Hall effect's TKNN invariant (Thouless-Kohmoto-Nightingale-den Nijs 1982).
realized_examples: Integer quantum Hall effect (von Klitzing 1980; classified by Chern number)

cells.description: Rank-2 free part; classified jointly by the signature and the c_1²/2 invariant (Chern character integrals).

closure_status.description: Partial. The cobordism-classification framework is established and proven; the explicit cobordism groups for the five tangential structures and small dimensions above are computed and standard. What remains partial: (i) higher dimensions and more exotic tangential structures (twisted Spin × G for various G) are not all explicitly computed; (ii) the framework's extension to non-invertible topological phases (true topological order) requires the modular-tensor-category machinery and remains an active research area; (iii) cosmological-scale generalizations (anomaly cobordism for gravity combined with arbitrary symmetry) are partially developed but incomplete. The 'partial' status reflects that the classification is closure-grade for the cases enumerated here but does not extend to the full landscape of SPT and TQFT phases.

predictive_yield.prediction: The free-fermion Z classification of 1d BDI topological superconductors collapses to Z_8 under interactions.
predictive_yield.confirmation_citation: Multiple independent theoretical confirmations (Turner-Pollmann-Berg 2011; Tang-Wen 2012; subsequent literature).

predictive_yield.prediction: Time-reversal-invariant 3+1d fermionic SPT phases with T² = -1 form a Z_16 classification when interactions are included, refining the Z_2 free-fermion classification of class AII.
predictive_yield.prediction_citation: Wang, Senthil 2014, Phys. Rev. B 89, 195124; Kapustin-Thorngren-Turzillo-Wang 2015, JHEP 12, 052; Freed-Hopkins 2016
predictive_yield.confirmation_citation: Theoretical: established via multiple frameworks. Experimental: partial — the higher-Z_16 phases require interaction-induced phases not yet isolated experimentally.

predictive_yield.prediction: The integer quantum Hall effect's TKNN invariant (first Chern number) is the Ω^{Spin^c}_2 = Z cobordism invariant; this provides a structural derivation rather than a phenomenological one.
predictive_yield.description: The IQHE was observed in 1980 (von Klitzing); the TKNN invariant interpretation came in 1982. The cobordism-theoretic re-derivation in Freed-Hopkins is retro-explanatory — it provides a uniform framework but did not predict the IQHE.

predictive_yield.prediction: The full classification of interacting fermionic SPT phases for arbitrary tangential structures in arbitrary dimensions reduces to computing cobordism groups Ω^G_{d+1}, a task that the algebraic-topology machinery (Adams spectral sequence, etc.) makes systematically computable.
predictive_yield.description: Methodological prediction. Not unconfirmed in the sense of being challenged, but the prediction is about a research program's reach; many cases remain to be computed even where the framework is accepted as correct.

citations: Freed, Hopkins 2016 (arXiv:1604.06527; published Comm. Math. Phys. 374, 1021 (2019)) — the foundational paper
Fidkowski, Kitaev 2010, Phys. Rev. B 81, 134509 (the Z → Z_8 collapse for 1d class BDI, demarcating the free-to-interacting reach)
Kapustin, Thorngren, Turzillo, Wang 2015, JHEP 12, 052 (independent cobordism-based classification)
Wang, Senthil 2014, Phys. Rev. B 89, 195124 (the Z_16 classification of 3+1d Pin+ phases)
Anderson, Brown, Peterson 1969, Ann. Math. 90, 1 (Spin bordism groups)
Kirby, Taylor 1990, in 'A Survey of 4-Manifolds Through the Eyes of Surgery' (Pin± bordism groups)

edges.description: The Freed-Hopkins cobordism classification is the most comprehensive answer to the topological-phases-classification frontier currently available. It subsumes the ten-fold way's free-fermion content, captures interaction-induced collapses (the canonical Z → Z_8 for 1d BDI) and refinements (Z_2 → Z_16 for 3+1d Pin+), and provides the general framework for invertible-phase classification. Nature: partially-solves — the framework solves the invertible-phase sub-domain of the frontier but does not address non-invertible topological order (anyons, fractional phases), which remains the open part of the topological-phases frontier.

subclaim_examples.free-fermion-sub-domain: substantial (via reduction to K-theory / ten-fold way)

citations: Senthil 2015, Annu. Rev. Condens. Matter Phys. 6, 299 (review of SPT classification and its open territory)

edges.description: Anomaly cobordism — the application of Freed-Hopkins-style cobordism classification to anomalies of quantum field theories — constrains which theories can be consistently coupled to gravity. The Witten anomaly (SU(2) global anomaly), the Bardeen-Zumino anomaly, and the cobordism interpretation of mixed gauge-gravitational anomalies all live in this framework. Nature: structural-only — the cobordism classification provides constraints on the QG landscape (anomaly-free combinations of gauge group + matter content) but does not solve the QG frontier itself. The relation to QG is via the anomaly side, not the gravitational dynamics side.
citations: Witten 1982, Phys. Lett. B 117, 324 (the SU(2) global anomaly)
García-Etxebarria, Montero 2019, JHEP 08, 003 (anomaly cobordism in QG and the swampland program)
Hsieh 2018, arXiv:1808.02881 (cobordism classification of SM-like anomalies)

edges.description: QFT uses the Freed-Hopkins classification at multiple levels: (i) as the framework for classifying invertible TQFTs in any dimension and tangential structure; (ii) as the framework for computing anomalies of fermionic gauge theories (anomaly cobordism); (iii) as the structural setting in which the relation between SPT phases and topological field theories is made precise. cell_refs pins the cells whose physical realizations are most prominent in QFT applications: the Arf invariant (Spin-d2), Fidkowski-Kitaev (Pin--d2), Z_16 (Pin+-d4), and IQHE/TKNN (Spin^c-d2).
citations: Freed 2014, in 'Mathematical Foundations of Quantum Field Theory' (Stanford Lectures)

edges.description: Statistical mechanics (specifically, condensed-matter many-body physics) uses Freed-Hopkins to classify gapped SPT phases beyond the free-fermion regime. The framework is the standard tool for analyzing whether two ground states of a gapped Hamiltonian are continuously connected (in the same SPT phase) or topologically distinct. cell_refs pins the cells with the most-cited condensed-matter realizations.
citations: Senthil 2015, Annu. Rev. Condens. Matter Phys. 6, 299

edges.description: The ten-fold way is the free-fermion specialization of the Freed-Hopkins cobordism classification. Each ten-fold-way symmetry class corresponds to a specific tangential structure plus internal symmetry combination in the Freed-Hopkins framework (e.g., class AII ↔ Pin+; class BDI ↔ Pin-; class A ↔ Spin^c; etc.), and the ten-fold-way's per-(class, dim) topological invariant (0, Z, Z_2, 2Z) is the FREE part or the Z_2-torsion part of the corresponding cobordism group Ω^G_{d+1}. The full cobordism group is in general LARGER (Z_8 vs Z for BDI in 1d; Z_16 vs Z_2 for AII in 4d) because the cobordism classification includes interaction-induced refinements that the free-fermion ten-fold way cannot see. cell_refs pins all 30 ten-fold-way cells (the entire ten-fold-way classification is what specializes). First use of the `specializes` cross-classification subtype in the project; the relation is canonically the example used in v14.1 findings §10.1 to motivate v15's typed cross-classification edges.
citations: Kitaev 2009, AIP Conf. Proc. 1134, 22 (ten-fold way; K-theory framing)
Freed, Hopkins 2016 (cobordism framing; recovers ten-fold way as free-fermion specialization)
Fidkowski, Kitaev 2010 (canonical example of where ten-fold way and Freed-Hopkins differ: the Z → Z_8 collapse demarcates the specialization)
